coredata.dc:title: Simple preparation of maltose-functionalized dendrimer/graphene quantum dots as a pH-sensitive biocompatible carrier for targeted delivery of doxorubicin
coredata.dc:creator: Karimi, Soheyla
Namazi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, a novel magnetic carbon modified with 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS) using maltose disaccharide molecule as a green capping agent, and a third-generation triazine dendrimer (Fe3O4@C@TD-G3) was then covalently attached to their surface. Eventually, Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 was reacted with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the preparation of final Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. The obtained compound was successfully applied as a novel magnetic carrier for the Doxorubicin (DOX) drug delivery. Various techniques such as Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Zeta potential, Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis), Atomic force microscopy (AFM), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), Vibration sample magnetometer (VSM), and Fluorescence and Photoluminescence (PL) analysis were used to approve the synthesis of microspheres. Drug release studies carried out at different pHs (pH 5, 6.8 and 7.4) and the cytotoxic assay was evaluated for DOX-loaded Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs against Human lung cancer cell lines (A549). The drug release assay showed that the amount of the DOX release from dendrimer was noticeably pH depended. In-vitro cytotoxicity test results indicated that DOX loaded Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs was non-toxic on the A549 cell. The obtained results demonstrated that Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres can be used as a new safe and efficient vehicle for the delivery of different cancer drugs.

head.ce:title: Simple preparation of maltose-functionalized dendrimer/graphene quantum dots as a pH-sensitive biocompatible carrier for targeted delivery of doxorubicin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, a novel magnetic carbon modified with 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS) using maltose disaccharide molecule as a green capping agent, and a third-generation triazine dendrimer (Fe3O4@C@TD-G3) was then covalently attached to their surface. Eventually, Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 was reacted with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the preparation of final Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. The obtained compound was successfully applied as a novel magnetic carrier for the Doxorubicin (DOX) drug delivery. Various techniques such as Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Zeta potential, Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis), Atomic force microscopy (AFM), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), Vibration sample magnetometer (VSM), and Fluorescence and Photoluminescence (PL) analysis were used to approve the synthesis of microspheres. Drug release studies carried out at different pHs (pH 5, 6.8 and 7.4) and the cytotoxic assay was evaluated for DOX-loaded Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs against Human lung cancer cell lines (A549). The drug release assay showed that the amount of the DOX release from dendrimer was noticeably pH depended. In-vitro cytotoxicity test results indicated that DOX loaded Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs was non-toxic on the A549 cell. The obtained results demonstrated that Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres can be used as a new safe and efficient vehicle for the delivery of different cancer drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a leading cause of human morbidity and death has attracted substantial research interest in the past several decades [1,2]. Among the various cancer treatment options, chemotherapy illustrates the main reason for the improved survival rates [3,4]. Numerous anticancer drugs such as 5-fluorouracil (5FU), doxorubicin (DOX), 6-mercaptopurine, paclitaxel, and procainamide hydrochloride have been used in cancer treatment [5]. Among these DOX, one of the cationic anticancer drugs, has been widely applied in the treatment of cancer during the last few years [6,7]. However, the remarkable side effects and systemic toxicity recorded by chemotherapeutic drugs mainly due to their lack of selectivity for cancer tissues and cells, elimination by the immune system, short blood half-life, and nonspecific targeting, often leads to the failure of chemotherapy [8,9]. In order to remove these side effects, nanocarriers can be used as drug carriers in drug delivery systems [10]. In the past few decades, many research attempts have been made to develop new drug delivery systems for drug delivery. Drug delivery can be achieved by using various drug carriers such as polymeric micelles, microspheres, nanogels, liposomes, xerogels, hydrogels, hydroxyapatite, polymers, dendrimers, quantum dots, silicon or carbon materials, mesoporous silica nanoparticles, and iron oxide magnetic nanoparticles [11–15]. The use of novel magnetic nanoparticles, especially Fe3O4 as a carrier system for bioactive molecules and drugs has been studied for the past several years due to their unique physical and chemical properties such as multi-functionality, biodegradability, superparamagnetism, good recoverability, high selectivity, non-cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, large pore volume, high surface area, high chemical stability, reactive surface, easy synthesis, and low cost of production [16–18]. Moreover, due to dipole-dipole interaction and high surface area, magnetic particles are easy to aggregate in aqueous solution, which reduced the drug delivery capacity [19,20]. One way to overcome this problem is to use a protective layer as a stabilizer and coating material, which prevents direct contact between the nanoparticles [21]. Hence, it is very important to formulating protection strategies for the chemical stabilization of magnetic nanoparticles by the forming inactive coating using inert materials such as novel macromolecules and inorganic materials or organic polymers (maltose) [22–24]. Therefore, maltose layer deposition on the magnetic core is commonly applied as the maltose surface can be easily functionalized with various organic compounds [25,26]. Out of many stabilizers, dendrimers are being used for modifying the surface of magnetic nanoparticles [27–29]. Dendrimers a unique class of macromolecules with internal pockets, monodispersed, biocompatibility, three-dimensional, and hyperbranched structure are being applied for modifying the surface of magnetic nanoparticles [30–34]. Creating functional groups, nanosize, water-solubility, safety, simplicity of use, biodegradability, good biocompatibility, immune compatibility, and the internal cavities on dendrimer-modified magnetic nanoparticles makes them appropriate for applications in drug delivery, biomedicine, biomaterial, also in catalysis and etc. [35–38]. In addition to these properties, the functionalization of a dendrimer with Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) could be leading to the superiority of good dispersibility, hyperbranched nanoarchitecture, and high capacity for drug delivery [39–42]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene nanosheets with unique properties such as eco-friendly, low-toxicity, chemical stability, high biocompatibility, good solubility, and highly tunable photoluminescence properties have recently been received much attention in the drug delivery system as delivery vehicles [43–45]. For example, PPy/mSiO2/GQDs were used as a new nanocarrier for the NIR irradiation-controlled release of MTX [46]. Up to now, numerous studies have been performed on the drug delivery field based on nanoparticle-cored dendrimer. PETIM dendrimer was used as a new carrier for the delivery of model drug ketoprofen [47]. The new CS-PAMAM was prepared for the delivery of anticancer drugs methotrexate [48]. In another work, PAMAM-dendrimer functionalized halloysite nanotubes were prepared as a new carrier for chlorogenic acid, ibuprofen and salicylic acid [49]. G4.0-PAMAM@HEP-mPEG nanocarriers were prepared and applied for DOX delivery [50]. The GO-CD-DEN was prepared as a new nanocarrier for the delivery of anticancer drugs such as chemotherapeutics (DOX and camptothecin) [51]. But to this day, no work has been published on the synthesis and application of magnetic nanoparticles supported GQDs modified triazine dendrimer a novel carrier for the delivery of anticancer drug DOX.

Therefore, in this work, different generations of novel magnetic nanoparticles supported GQDs modified triazine dendrimer were synthesized for the first time through magnetic separation technology and step-by-step chemical graft method. The structure characteristics of the prepared dendrimer were studied by FTIR, XRD, SEM, EDX, Zeta potential, AFM, UV–visible, BET, VSM, Fluorescence and Photoluminescence (PL) analysis. Finally, the prepared dendrimer was used as a new carrier for DOX delivery. As well as, drug release in vitro and a cytotoxic assay of the dendrimer were evaluated.

All chemicals used in this work such as Ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2. 4H2O), Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3. 6H2O), Ethanol, Methanol, Malic acid, Melamine, 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), Ammonia (NH3, 25%), 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Potassium chloride (KCl), Dry toluene, Sodium chloride (NaCl), Monopotassium phosphate (KH2PO4), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4), Sodium acetate, Acetic acid, Citric acid monohydrate (CA), and Maltose were purchased from Merck. N, N-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazoliumbromide (MTT) were prepared from Sigma-Aldrich. Furthermore, other materials which in used in biological protocols including Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Penicillin streptomycin (PS) and Roswell Park Memorial Institute 1640 growth medium (RPMI) were prepared from Gibco BRL Life Technologies. The Doxorubicin (DOX) was obtained from Sobhan Pharmaceuticals Co. (Iran). Deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on a Bruker Aquinox 55 spectrometer in 400–4000 cm−1 range using KBr pellets. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurements of samples were carried out by a Bruker Advance D8 X-ray diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation at room temperature at 35 kV in 2θ = 10–80°. The sample's surface morphology was investigated using an LEO 1430VP electron microscope (SEM). Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was performed by a FESEM-EDX spectrometer. The magnetic properties of the carriers were studied using an MDKF vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) at room temperature. The pore size distribution and the specific surface area (SBET) were obtained by Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) methods, respectively. The Electrophoretic light scattering (ELS) measurements were performed to determine the Zeta potential of the prepared dendrimer. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) observation was investigated using a Digital Instruments NanoSurf apparatus. UV–vis spectra were recorded by the Analytikjena UV–vis spectrometer. The fluorescence and Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed on an LS-45 Perkin Elmer fluorescence spectrophotometer. Sonication was performed on an ultrasonic processor. A digital pH meter, PHS-3C pH meter was used for pH adjustments.

In this work, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized through modification of the previously published co-precipitation method [52]. First, 4.3 g of FeCl2.4H2O and 11.68 g of FeCl3.6H2O were dissolved in 200 mL of deionized water at 80–85 °C under N2 atmosphere for 30 min. Then, 20 mL of 25% NH4OH was rapidly added drop-wise into the reaction mixture, and vigorously stirred for 30 min. The obtained black precipitate was isolated by a strong external magnetic field and was washed twice with distilled water and twice with 0.02 M solution of NaCl.

In this work, Fe3O4@C was prepared according to the previously published [53] with a minor modification. Briefly, the prepared Fe3O4 (0.1 g) was dispersed in 30 mL distilled water containing 2 g maltose for 30 min. The resulting mixture was then transferred into an autoclave made of Teflon stainless steel, and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. The obtained black precipitate was separated from the solution by an external magnet, washed several times with distilled water, and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum for 24 h.

The functionalization of the obtained Fe3O4@C with APTMS to introduce the amino group on its surface was achieved according to the literature [53] with a minor modification. Briefly, as-prepared Fe3O4@C (200 mg) was dispersed in 10 mL toluene and then, 3 mL of APTMS was added drop-wise to the mixture. Then, the reaction mixture was refluxed at 110 °C for 24 h. Finally, the obtained product was separated by an external magnet and washed several times with ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4@C@NH2-supported G3 triazine dendritic polymer (Fe3O4@C@TD-G0–Fe3O4@C@TD-G3)
ce:section.ce:para: Step 1: Fe3O4@C@NH2 (0.12 g) was dispersed in 10 mL of dry toluene; the mixture was then ultrasonicated for 30 min. Then, 0.12 g of malic acid in 5 mL of dry toluene containing DCC (0.12 g) and DMAP (0.04 g) was added drop-wise into the mixture and it was then stirred at 25 °C for 12 h under nitrogen gas. The Fe3O4@C@TD-G0.5 were separated by an external magnetic field, washed twice with dry toluene, twice with ethanol and twice with methanol, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 65 °C.

Step 2: For preparation Fe3O4@C@NH2 supported G3 dendritic polymer, Fe3O4@C@TD-G0.5 (0.107 g) and DCC (1.12 g) and DMAP (0.1 g) were suspended in 50 mL of dry DMSO, and after sonication for 30 min, melamine (1.2 g) in 10 mL dry DMSO was added drop-wise into the mixture and the mixture was refluxed with continuous stirring under N2 atmosphere for 24 h. The product was collected by an external magnetic field, washed twice with water, twice with ethanol and twice with methanol and then dried in a vacuum oven at 65 °C. The repetition of the above-mentioned steps gave second- and third-generation dendrimers attached on the surface of Fe3O4@C@NH2.

The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared using the citric acid pyrolysis (CA) method as described in our previous work with minor modifications [54]. Briefly, 2 g of citric acid was heated to 200 °C using a heating mantle for 30 min. Then, the obtained orange liquid was added drop-wise to 100 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH at room temperature under vigorous stirring. After neutralized with HCl (pH 7), the stock solution of GQDs was obtained.

The conjugation of GQDs with amino-functionalized dendrimer was performed by the cross-linking reaction between carboxyl groups of GQDs and amine groups of the dendrimer [55]. Briefly, the obtained Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 dendrimer was then dispersed in 100 mL DMSO, in which 0.8 g of GQDs were activated by 100 mg of DCC and 70 mg of DMAP in a dark environment. The mixture was subsequently stirred for 1 h to obtain core-shell structural Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. The composites were collected by an external magnetic field and then washed several times with water, ethanol, and methanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading and release in Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres
ce:section.ce:para:
Loading of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs carrier with DOX drug was carried out as follows: Briefly, 40 mg of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs was dispersed in 40 mL of DOX solution (100 ppm in PBS, pH 7.4) for 10 min, and the suspension was shacked on a shaker for 72 h in the dark. By using an external magnetic field drug-loaded Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs was separated from the mixture and washed with a minimum amount of PBS to remove the weakly adsorbed drug molecules. Then, the amount of loaded drug in the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs was determined using a UV–vis spectroscopy at 480 nm. The drug loading capacity (DLC) and entrapment efficiency (EE) of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX were calculated using the following:
(1)
DLC % =
Mass of drug in nanocarrier
mg
Mass of nanocarrier
mg
× 100
(2)
EE % =
Mass of drug in nanocarrier
mg
Mass of feed drug
mg
× 100

The release of DOX from Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres in PBS at different pH levels (pH 5, 6.8 and 7.4) was studied. For this work, 10 mg of DOX loaded dendrimer was dispersed in 10 mL buffer solutions of pH 5.5, 6.8 and 7.4 and given to continuous shaking under dark conditions at 37 °C for different periods of time. At specific time intervals, 3 mL of supernatant from the sample was removed, and replaced with equivalent volumes of fresh PBS for continuous drug release. The amount of released DOX from the dendrimer nanocomposite was evaluated by UV–vis spectroscopy. All the tests were conducted in triplicate. The cumulative percentage of the released drug was calculated by the Eq. (3):
(3)
RD % =
D R
D T
× 100
where DR is the weight of the drug released at collected time (t) (mg) and DT is the total weight of the drug that was encapsulated in the dendrimer (mg).

DOX adsorption experiments on Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs were carried out as follows: 40 mg of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs added into 40 mL of DOX solution with a known initial concentration from 50 to 250 mg/L. The suspension was shaken at room temperature for 72 h in the dark. The drug encapsulated Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres were separated in the presence of an external magnetic field and the supernatant concentration was determined using the UV–vis spectrophotometer at 480 nm. The adsorption amount of DOX was calculated by Eq. (4).
(4)
Qe =
C 0 − Ce
M
× V
where Qe is the equilibrium adsorption capacity of DOX on the adsorbent (mg/g), C0 and Ce are the initial and equilibrium concentration (mg/L) of DOX in solution, respectively, M is the mass of adsorbent (g), V is the volume of DOX solution (L).

The cytotoxicity of the GQDs conjugated Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 without and with DOX drug was assessed by the standard method 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazoliumbromide (MTT) assay on Human lung cancer cell lines (A549 cells). Typically, A549 cells were seeded in 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells per well) and cultured in the RPMI-1640 medium and DMEM medium containing 10% of fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% penicillin and 1% streptomycin in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. After the interval, the culture medium was removed and replaced with 200 mL of different concentrations (32, 16, 8, 4, and 2 μg/mL) of DOX, Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX, Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs suspensions and incubated continually for 72 h at 37 °C in the dark. Then, 20 μL MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was poured into each well and further incubated at 37 °C for 4 h at a dark place, washed with PBS (250 μL of 0.1 M, pH 7.4). The MTT medium was carefully removed from wells and 200 μL of DMSO was added to each well to dissolve blue formazan crystals. The absorbance of the solution was measured by an ELISA plate reader at 570 nm by a reference wavelength of 630 nm, and the percentage growth inhibition was obtained. All cytotoxicity tests were performed in triplicate. Cell viability (%) was calculated by the following equation:
(5)
Cell viability
%
=
optical density of the cells treated with samples
optical density of the control
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and biodegradation of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the colloidal stability (aggregation) of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres at 1% concentrations magnetic nanocarriers were evaluated by recording the change in turbidity in each solution (purified water, PBS (pH = 7.4), FBS and DMEM).

In the following, the in-vitro biodegradation of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres to evaluate the stability of the prepared dendrimer was studied. First, 10 mg of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres were dispersed in PBS (5 mL) at pH 7.4. After, Lysozyme solution (0.5 mL, 5% w) was added to the suspension. The in-vitro biodegradation study of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres was carried out at 37 °C in an orbital shaker at 50 rpm at different times (0–72 h). Then, the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres were removed from the reaction medium with an external magnetic field and washed several times with deionized water. After vacuum drying at 60 °C, the remained Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres were accurately weighted. The weight loss percentage (ΔW%) at each time interval was obtained using the following formula:
(6)
W loss
%
=
W 0
−
W t
W 0
× 100
where W0 is the initial weight before degradation, and Wt is the weight of the sample after biodegradation. Each biodegradation experiment were performed in triplicate.

Firstly, magnetic nanoparticles were prepared by the chemical co-precipitation method. Following, we encapsulated these nanoparticles with carbon structure using maltose as a carbon source by a hydrothermal treatment [53]. Due to the fact that the surface of the existing carbon structure is covered with numerous hydroxyl groups, then it can be functionalized with APTMS. Finally, the Fe3O4@C@NH2 reacted with malic acid which produced the 0.5 generation of triazine dendrimer supported on Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4@C@TD-G0.5). In the next step, Fe3O4@C@TD-G0.5 dendrimer reacted with melamine to generate the G.1 generation of Fe3O4@C@TD dendrimer. The repetition of these two steps produced the third generation of the Fe3O4@C@TD dendrimer. After generating Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 dendrimer, GQDs was reacted with Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 dendrimer which produced the of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres as a potential platform for loading of anticancer drug DOX. The successful synthesis of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres was approved by FT-IR, XRD, Zeta potential, UV–visible, AFM, SEM, EDX, BET, VSM, Fluorescence and Photoluminescence (PL) analyses. The synthetic route of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres is shown in Scheme 1 .

The successful growth of dendrimer on Fe3O4@C support was further confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 1a shows the FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C, Fe3O4@C-NH2, G1, G2, and G3 generation of Fe3O4@C@TD, and Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. The FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4 NPs shows a sharp absorption band at 584 cm−1, corresponding to the FeO bending vibration. The bands around 3434 and 1631 cm−1 are due to the OH vibration of adsorption water. As shown in Fig. 1a, the spectra of Fe3O4@C exhibited similar peaks at 1698 and 1635 cm−1, which were attributed to CO and CC vibrations, respectively, supporting the concept of maltose aromatization during the hydrothermal method. In addition, the bands in the 1000–1300 cm−1 range were related to CO stretching and OH bending vibrations, indicating the existence of a large number of hydroxyl groups remaining on their surface. The FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4@C-NH2 shows weak bands at 2922 and 1526 cm−1 are corresponding to the CH stretching of propyl groups and amine groups, respectively, which confirmed the binding of APTMS molecules at the surface of Fe3O4@C [53]. The grafting of a dendrimer with generation number G0.5-G3 onto the surface of Fe3O4@C-NH2 was performed via amidation reactions by sequential growing technique. The FT-IR spectrum of G 0.5, shows three new peaks at 1205, 1380, and 1559 cm−1 corresponding to NH, CN, and CONH, respectively and thus strongly indicates the formation of the amide group (Fig. 1S, available in Electronic Supplementary Material). In the G1, the peaks at 1557,1642 and 3397 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of CN, CONH and OH, respectively [56]. NH wag of NH2 triazine diamine functionalized dendrimer appeared at 802 cm−1, indicating the presence of the melamine in the dendrimer. In G1.5, the appearance of new bands at 1397 and 1700 cm−1 indicates the stretching and bending vibration of the carboxyl group (COOH). It is also observed that the NH2 group appeared at 802 cm−1 disappeared. Therefore, the appearance of the COOH group and the disappearance of NH2 functional group confirms the occurrence of malic acid addition on G1 (Fig. 1S, available in Electronic Supplementary Material). FT-IR spectral analysis of G2 shows the band at 1557, 1638 cm−1 due to bending and stretching vibrations of NH and CONH, respectively. On comparing the FT-IR of the spectrum of the G2 generation matrix with G2.5 it is observed that the peaks appeared at 800 cm−1 for NH2 wag of triazine diamine-functionalized dendrimer disappeared and a two peak appeared at 1562 and 1635 cm−1 indicating the conversion of amine group into amides. Similarly, another characteristic peak viz., COOH group noticed in Fig. 1S (available in Electronic Supplementary Material) at 1397 and 1700 cm−1 confirms the occurrence of amidation reaction. Finally, the FT-IR spectrum of G3 shows a new band at 800, 1557, 1638 cm−1 due to wag, bending and stretching vibrations of NH2, NH and CONH, respectively, and thus strongly indicates the formation of the amide group. The increase in the relative intensity of the above peaks of G0.5 to G3 indicates that the dendrimers were successfully fabricated at Fe3O4@C-NH2 surface. Thus, all the above results indicate that dendrimers have been grafted successfully on Fe3O4@C-NH2. Further, the FT-IR of GQDs in Fig. 1S (available in Electronic Supplementary Material) shows peaks at 1725 and 1404 cm−1 due to CO and C-OH stretching vibration of COOH groups. Other peaks are observed at 1567 and 1084 cm−1 due to CC and COH stretching vibrations, respectively. After conjugated GQDs with dendrimer, the resultant Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs showed the new peak at 1557, 1638 and 1701 cm−1 were attributed to the NH, CONH and CO, respectively [57], which demonstrated that the dendrimer has been grafted onto the flakes of GQDs.

The successful growth of dendrimer on Fe3O4@C support was further confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 1a shows the FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C, Fe3O4@C-NH2, G1, G2, and G3 generation of Fe3O4@C@TD, and Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. The FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4 NPs shows a sharp absorption band at 584 cm−1, corresponding to the FeO bending vibration. The bands around 3434 and 1631 cm−1 are due to the OH vibration of adsorption water. As shown in Fig. 1a, the spectra of Fe3O4@C exhibited similar peaks at 1698 and 1635 cm−1, which were attributed to CO and CC vibrations, respectively, supporting the concept of maltose aromatization during the hydrothermal method. In addition, the bands in the 1000–1300 cm−1 range were related to CO stretching and OH bending vibrations, indicating the existence of a large number of hydroxyl groups remaining on their surface. The FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4@C-NH2 shows weak bands at 2922 and 1526 cm−1 are corresponding to the CH stretching of propyl groups and amine groups, respectively, which confirmed the binding of APTMS molecules at the surface of Fe3O4@C [53]. The grafting of a dendrimer with generation number G0.5-G3 onto the surface of Fe3O4@C-NH2 was performed via amidation reactions by sequential growing technique. The FT-IR spectrum of G 0.5, shows three new peaks at 1205, 1380, and 1559 cm−1 corresponding to NH, CN, and CONH, respectively and thus strongly indicates the formation of the amide group (Fig. 1S, available in Electronic Supplementary Material). In the G1, the peaks at 1557,1642 and 3397 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of CN, CONH and OH, respectively [56]. NH wag of NH2 triazine diamine functionalized dendrimer appeared at 802 cm−1, indicating the presence of the melamine in the dendrimer. In G1.5, the appearance of new bands at 1397 and 1700 cm−1 indicates the stretching and bending vibration of the carboxyl group (COOH). It is also observed that the NH2 group appeared at 802 cm−1 disappeared. Therefore, the appearance of the COOH group and the disappearance of NH2 functional group confirms the occurrence of malic acid addition on G1 (Fig. 1S, available in Electronic Supplementary Material). FT-IR spectral analysis of G2 shows the band at 1557, 1638 cm−1 due to bending and stretching vibrations of NH and CONH, respectively. On comparing the FT-IR of the spectrum of the G2 generation matrix with G2.5 it is observed that the peaks appeared at 800 cm−1 for NH2 wag of triazine diamine-functionalized dendrimer disappeared and a two peak appeared at 1562 and 1635 cm−1 indicating the conversion of amine group into amides. Similarly, another characteristic peak viz., COOH group noticed in Fig. 1S (available in Electronic Supplementary Material) at 1397 and 1700 cm−1 confirms the occurrence of amidation reaction. Finally, the FT-IR spectrum of G3 shows a new band at 800, 1557, 1638 cm−1 due to wag, bending and stretching vibrations of NH2, NH and CONH, respectively, and thus strongly indicates the formation of the amide group. The increase in the relative intensity of the above peaks of G0.5 to G3 indicates that the dendrimers were successfully fabricated at Fe3O4@C-NH2 surface. Thus, all the above results indicate that dendrimers have been grafted successfully on Fe3O4@C-NH2. Further, the FT-IR of GQDs in Fig. 1S (available in Electronic Supplementary Material) shows peaks at 1725 and 1404 cm−1 due to CO and C-OH stretching vibration of COOH groups. Other peaks are observed at 1567 and 1084 cm−1 due to CC and COH stretching vibrations, respectively. After conjugated GQDs with dendrimer, the resultant Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs showed the new peak at 1557, 1638 and 1701 cm−1 were attributed to the NH, CONH and CO, respectively [57], which demonstrated that the dendrimer has been grafted onto the flakes of GQDs.

The crystalline structure of the Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C, Fe3O4@C@TD-G3, and Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres were characterized by XRD measurements as shown in Fig. 1b. The XRD spectra of Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 dendrimer is similar to that of Fe3O4 and there was no essential change in the topological structure of Fe3O4 before and after the functionalization, implying Fe3O4 has excellent mechanical stability. Also, the result showed that the encapsulation of Fe3O4 with carbon shell has decreased the intensity of the peaks which confirms the insertion of a non-crystalline structure (herein carbon shell) [53]. The XRD data of the prepared Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 display diffraction peaks at 2θ = 30.1°, 35.4°, 43.1°, 53.4°, 57.3°, 62.6° and 74.5° which can be related to the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440) and (533) planes of Fe3O4, respectively, indicating that the Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 did not result in the crystallinity change of pure Fe3O4 with a cubic spinel structure. Compared with Fe3O4, in Fig. 1b of Fe3O4@C@TD-G3, the characteristic peaks intensity of Fe3O4 are all weakened, indicating dendrimer was successfully grafted on the surface of Fe3O4@C and the crystalline structure of Fe3O4 did not change in the process of grafting. The peak at 2θ = 22.5° in Fe3O4@C@TD-G3, which is attributed to the dendrimer layer is a confirmation for the successful synthesis of the dendrimer layer [58]. The XRD pattern of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres shows a wide (002) peak at 2θ = 22.6°, which was in good agreement with the interlayer distance of graphene sheets, indicating the graphitic nature of the crystalline dots [40,59]. The average particle size of dendrimer nanoparticles was also determined from XRD data by the Debye-Scherrer equation:
(7)
D =
Kλ βCosθ
where D is the average size of nanoparticles, K is Scherrer constant (0.89), λ is the wavelength of the X-ray wave used, β is the full width at half maximum value of the diffraction peak, and θ is the angle of diffraction to the peak. The average crystalline size of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C, Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 and Fe3O4@C@TDGQD microspheres were ~30.9, 109.9, 111.5 and 112.4 nm, respectively.

The zeta potential of Fe3O4@C@TD-G3, GQDs, Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs, DOX, and Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX was determined using Electrophoretic light scattering (ELS). Zeta potential measurements permit us to determine the surface charge and the stability of the synthesized material. At higher zeta potential, the system is more stable, which helps to play the function of drug delivery. Also, the high positive or negative zeta potential value in the suspension reduces the particle aggregation behavior. Hence, to confirm the drug delivery ability of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs, Fig. 2a shows the zeta potential results of Fe3O4@C@TD-G3, GQDs, Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs, DOX, and Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX. As can be seen in Fig. 2a, the zeta potential of GQDs is about −20.8 mV due to its surface with COOH. For Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 due to a lot of NH2, its zeta potential showed as +36.34 mV. After dendrimer G3 was conjugated on GQDs, the zeta potential of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs is about −55.44 mV due to the decreased the number of the amino groups of triazine dendrimer by amidation with COOH groups from GQDs. Consequently, it is expected that the negatively charged GQDs will be wrapped with highly positively charged dendrimer via the electrostatic attractions. On the other hand, the H-bonding associations between the NH2/OH groups of dendrimer and the OH/COOH groups of GQDs are also useful for the formation of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs. These results confirm the successful functionalization and preparation of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. After DOX was loaded onto Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs, the surface potential of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX was −38.44 mV, which was due to the adsorption of DOX onto the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs surface [60].

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres is shown in Fig. 2b. By the classification of hysteresis loops given by the IUPAC, the curve of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs is exhibited as a type IV isotherm with a type H1 hysteresis loop. The IV type of nitrogen adsorption isotherm indicates Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs has the characteristic of a mesoporous material with one-dimensional cylindrical channels. Table 1 summarizes the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm (BET) and pores size distribution (BJH) parameters of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. The BET specific surface area was 5.2 m2/g, total pore volume was 0.033 cm3/g and the average pore size of the dendrimer was 25.6 nm which is useful in drug delivery. The reason for this result can be attributed to the formation of the dendrimer structure in the pore channels of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [61].

The AFM image of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres with a corresponding line scan is shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3 shows that the carrier has a relatively rough surface due to grafting dendrimer on the surface of the GQDs [59]. The average height of prepared microspheres is found to be 189.2 nm. The obtained results confirmed the successful modification of the Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 dendrimer with GQDs.

The morphology and particle size of the Fe3O4@C, Fe3O4@C@TD-G3, Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs, and Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX microspheres were studied by SEM and the images are shown in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a, the spherical Fe3O4@C microspheres were successfully synthesized and the diameter was about 2–3 μm. The SEM image of Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 in Fig. 4b revealed the surface morphology of nanoparticles with a spherical shape. After surface grafting Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 with GQDs, these microspheres still retained their spherical shape (Fig. 4c). The SEM image of the DOX drug-loaded Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres are shown in Fig. 4d. As presented in Fig. 4d, the image of DOX drug-loaded Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres showed the spherical-like crystalline nature of the sample. This results in surface morphology showed the successful formation of the inclusion complex of DOX with nanocavity of GQDs or dendritic network of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres.

To estimate the relative quantity of the main elements of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres including C, N, O, Fe and Si, EDX measurements were performed to confirm their elemental compositions (Fig. 4e). As shown in Fig. 4e, The peak of Fe is attributed to Fe3O4, the peak of O is attributed to Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C, dendrimer and GQDs, The peak of C is attributed to Fe3O4@C, dendrimer, and GQDs, the peak of N is attributed to amino functional groups in dendrimer [62]. This result indicated that the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres were successfully prepared with the proposed process.

The magnetic property of pure Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C, Fe3O4@C-NH2, and Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres was studied by measuring magnetization at room temperature as shown in Fig. 5a. The specific saturation magnetization (Ms) of pure Fe3O4, Fe3O4@C, Fe3O4@C-NH2, and Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres was found to be at 67, 45.5, 36.4 and 20.4 emu/g, respectively, which were lower than bare magnetic nanoparticles, due to the fact that the incorporation dendrimer functional groups on the surface decreased the intensity of magnetization of Fe3O4. Accordingly, the decrease in the amount of magnetization for Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres can be explained by the larger group of maltose, GQDs, and non-magnetic triazine dendrimer on the surface of Fe3O4 [56]. Anyway, the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs is still strong enough to be separated and collected from the mixture solution under the external magnet within 30 s and also to be re-dispersed immediately of the solution under ultrasonication. The obtained result suggesting that Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres exhibited superparamagnetic behavior.

Fig. 5b shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3O4@C@TD-G3, GQDs, Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs, DOX, and Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX. For Fe3O4@C@TD-G3 dendrimer, the maximum absorption band at 231 and 383 nm is attributed to the n-π* electronic transitions of the aromatic CN bonds and n-π* transitions of the CO bonds in the dendrimer, respectively. The UV–vis absorption of GQDs displayed three clear peaks at 230 nm, 276 nm and 360 nm, which was attributed to the n → π* (CC) and π → π* (CO) transitions [40,63]. After conjugation with GQDs, the absorption spectrum of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs shows the peaks of GQDs and dendrimer at 230 nm and 360 nm, respectively, indicating the successful formation of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. Further, the specific UV–vis absorption peak of free DOX in Fig. 5b showed absorption bands at 255, 293 and 489 nm [64]. After the loading of DOX on Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres, the UV–vis absorption of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs included obvious peaks at 255 nm, 293 and 489 nm, which are corresponded to the peak of free DOX, demonstrating that DOX molecule has been successfully loaded onto Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres.

Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission spectra of synthesized Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres were illustrated in Fig. 5c. Upon illumination with UV light, synthesized GQDs exhibit blue photoluminescence with strong excitation/emission peaks at 365/460 nm [40,65]. The as-prepared Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres display bright blue fluorescence with excitation/emission maxima of 365/455 nm. As shown in Fig. 5c, the red shift in the peak emission of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs indicates the incorporation of the GQDs into the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. The obtained result showed that these Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres can be used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of biological samples (350–420 nm). In addition, DOX is a well-known fluorescent drug molecule with a strong emission band at 588 nm under excitation at 365 nm. The fluorescence intensity of DOX decreased in the DOX loaded Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs system from 588 to 455 nm as seen in Fig. 5c, thus indicating that a chemical interaction occurred between the drug and Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading and release behaviors of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: In order to examine the capacity of the as-prepared Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres as a novel carrier for the delivery and loading of therapeutic molecules, DOX was selected as a model anticancer drug. In this work, the loading of drugs carried out by mixing Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs with DOX solution and then shaking for 72 h in the dark. The amount of loaded DOX into the dendrimer was calculated by using UV–vis spectra analysis at 480 nm. The drug loading (DLC) and entrapment efficiency (EE) of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres were found to be 63.09% and 63.09%, respectively. The high drug loading capacity of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres can be related to five factors: 1) Different interactions such as π-π interaction, electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding of positively charged DOX molecules with negatively charged dendrimer nanocarrier. The π-π interaction between the aromatic rings in the carbon shell of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres and the conjugated rings of DOX molecules; the hydrogen bonding between the surface OH/COOH groups of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres and the NH2/OH groups of DOX; and the electrostatic attractions between the acidic phenolic hydroxyl and alkaline amino groups of DOX and carboxyl and OH groups of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. 2) The delicate three-dimensional structure of the triazine dendrimer with very high branches and cavities leads to high drug loading. 3) High loading amount of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres could be attributed to entrapment of DOX with dendritic network and the inclusion of the GQDs cavity to the drug. 4) The hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic effects between triazine dendrimer and DOX lead to more retention of the drug within the dendrimer cavities, and thus, the high capacity of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs carrier for DOX acceptance. 5) The unique structure with a large central hollow cavity and pores in the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres provides ample storage space for the drug. Thus, these observations suggest that Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres can be utilized as a potential drug carrier.

The in vitro release behavior of DOX from Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres were investigated in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with different pH values (pH 5, pH 6.8 and pH 7.4) and different time intervals (2, 4, 8, 12, 24, 48 and 72 h) at 37 °C. Fig. 6a shows the DOX release profiles from Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres at different pH values. As seen in Fig. 6a, DOX releasing is time-dependent and pH-dependent with superior release observed at pH 5.5 compared to pH 7.4. In this case, approximately 65.09%, 35.32% and 28.48% of loaded DOX released at pH 5, 6.8 and 7.4 after 72 h, respectively. This observation can be explained as DOX (pKa ~8.3) exists as a positively charged ion at both pH 7.4 and 5.5. As well at pH 7.4, the COOH and OH groups of GQDs moiety of Fe3O4@C@TD exist as COO and other hydrophilic parts of dendrimer remain in the unprotonated state. For this reason, at pH 7.4, Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs may form stronger interaction with positively charged DOX and show sustained release behavior. Also, sustained release can be achieved due to drug diffusion from the GQDs cavity and the dendrimer network. While the hydrophilic moiety of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs protonated at pH 5.5, which forms a repulsive interaction with the positively charged DOX, resulting in a relatively faster release of the drug at pH 5.5. Due to high DOX release at acidic pH, the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX has more advantage to the treatment of cancer cells.

Adsorption isotherms were studied to describe the equilibrium adsorption behavior of adsorbed molecules. In this study, two well-known models, the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were thereby applied to analyze the equilibrium data. The Langmuir isotherm is valid for monolayer adsorption on the homogenous and uniform surface of adsorbent with a finite number of identical sites. The linear form of the Langmuir equation can be expressed as follows:
(8)
Ce Qe
=
Ce Qm
+
1
KL × Qm
where Qe is the equilibrium adsorption capacity of DOX on the adsorbent (mg/g), Ce is the equilibrium concentration (mg/L) of DOX in solution, Qm is maximum adsorption capacity of the adsorbent (mg/g), and KL is Langmuir adsorption constant. The Langmuir constants KL and Qm were calculated from the intercepts and slopes of the straight lines of Ce/Qe vs. Ce (Fig. 6b) and their values are listed in Table 2 .

The Freundlich model is an empirical equation that suggests the heterogeneity of the sorbent surface and multilayer formation. The linear form of the Freundlich equation can be represented as follows:
(9)
Ln
Qe = Ln Kf +
1 n
Ln
Ce
where n and Kf are the Freundlich constants and the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent, respectively. The values of 1/n can be classified as favorable (0 < 1/n < 1), unfavorable (1/n > 1), and irreversible (1/n = 0). The values of Kf and 1/n, as determined from the slope and intercept of the linear plot of Ln Qe versus Ln Ce (Fig. 6c).

According to Table 2, the regression correlation coefficients (R2) for the Freundlich model were 0.9621 while those for the Langmuir model were 0.8184. These results confirmed that the Freundlich model fitted better than the Langmuir model. In addition, the obtained values of 1/n confirm that the adsorption of DOX on the surface of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs is a favorable process.

A comparison of loading capacity (DLC%) of DOX on the different nanocarriers was presented in Table 3 . It can be seen from Table 3 that drug loading capacities of the synthesized dendrimer in this work for DOX drugs were better, acceptable, compatible, and comparable with some other nanocarriers reported from literature. Thus, it can be inferred that Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres can be a suitable controlled drug delivery system for cancer treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, cytotoxicity assay (MTT assay) was used to evaluate the potential and biocompatibility of the prepared dendrimer for using as a new drug carrier. To this end, the cytotoxicity of free DOX, Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs and Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX in various concentrations (2, 4, 8, 16 and 32 μg/mL) were studied against A549 cells. Fig. 7 shows the cell cytotoxicity results of each sample in comparison to a control. According to the cytotoxicity results of blank Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres, when the concentrations increased in rage 2–32 μg/mL, Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs will not cause significant cytotoxicity (>80% cell viability) on the A549 cell after 72 h. These indicate that Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres are safe and good biocompatibility for in drug delivery system uses. Also, Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX exhibited % cell viability as 47, 30, 18, 9 and 3% in concentrations rage of 2–32 μg/mL, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7, the cell survival rate was greatly reduced by increasing the concentrations of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX from 1 to 32 μg/mL. It indicates that the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX systems are effective to kill the cancer cells. Also, the results clearly showed that the DOX activity loaded on the dendrimer carrier was greater than that of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs and free DOX at all concentrations. The increased cytotoxicity of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs-DOX may be due to DOX efficient transport by microspheres through an endocytosis mechanism compared to the mechanism of passive diffusion of free DOX into cells [20]. From the MTT results, it can conclude that synthesized nanocarrier is biocompatible and can be used as a novel nanocarrier for drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and biodegradation behavior of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The colloidal stability of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres as a magnetic carrier was studied to better understand the aggregation and stability of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres in aqueous solution and facilitate the prediction of their fate in the physiological condition. The results showed that magnetic nanocarrier in 1% concentrations has no remarkable changes in behaviors and stability. This indicates that the colloidal stability (aggregation) of the magnetic carrier in physiological conditions is a complex function of microspheres surface properties and the surrounding environmental components. The results confirmed that the suspension with low rate sedimentation has excellent stability in which the aggregation can be easily decomposed by sonication or stirring. To demonstrate the carrier diffusing capacity, a droplet of carrier suspension was added to the simulated physiological condition medium with PBS, and a series of images was snapshotted. As shown in Fig. 8a, the carrier can easily disperse in the container containing PBS. Therefore, we found that Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres which were used as magnetic nanocarrier in this study, not only have no changes in behavior for a period but also have no stability changes and could easily be dispersed in physiological conditions.

In this study, the in vitro biodegradation behavior of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres was performed using the PBS-lysozyme solution as a hydrolase enzyme at 37 °C at different times. As seen in Fig. 8b, a large amount of in vitro enzymatic degradation of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres were obtained in the first 48 h. It seems that in the first 48 h, the degradation largely because of the degradation of GQDs and three generations of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. This results in a significant reduction in the organic weight of the carrier scaffold. In the process, other components of the dendrimer structure have been degraded. This result indicates the good biodegradability of the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres as a safe drug delivery system.

In this research, we have reported the synthesis and characterization of a new triazine dendrimer-conjugated GQDs immobilized on Fe3O4@C@NH2 core-shell microspheres (Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs) as a potential carrier for the DOX drug delivery. FTIR, XRD, SEM, EDX, Zeta potential, AFM, UV–visible, BET, VSM, Fluorescence and Photoluminescence (PL) analyses confirmed the successful preparation of synthesized dendrimer microspheres. The loading efficiency of DOX on Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres was found by about 63.09%. DOX-loaded Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres were exposed to pH changes to investigate the drug release behavior of Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres. The total amount of DOX released was approximately 65.09% under mildly acidic conditions (pH 5.0), whereas only 28.48% of DOX was released at almost neutral pH (7.4) after 72 h. The studies of the adsorption process suggested the adsorption of DOX fitted well to the Freundlich isotherm models. MTT results revealed that the prepared Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres have no toxicity against A549 cell lines in comparison to the free drug. Furthermore, the Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres demonstrated good stability and considerable enzymatical degradation as a safe drug delivery system. All obtained results indicate that the prepared Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs microspheres can be used as a new safe, biocompatible, and effective nanocarrier for biomedical applications.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. 1S
FT-IR spectra of a) Fe3O4, b) Fe3O4@C, c) Fe3O4@C-NH2, d) G0.5-G3 generation of triazine dendrimer supported on magnetic nanoparticles, e) GQDs, and g) Fe3O4@C@TDGQDs.
Fig. 1S

Soheyla Karimi carried out the experiment. Soheyla Karimi wrote the manuscript with support from Hassan Namazi. Hassan Namazi supervised the project. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.
